title: Computer Vision Tasks
learning_outcomes: Formulate the object detection pipeline in single-stage models like YOLO, defining bounding box coordinates and objectness scores.
Calculate the Intersection over Union (IoU) between bounding boxes and formulate the Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) sorting loop.
Explain the symmetrical encoder-decoder structure of U-Net and prove how skip connections recover high-resolution spatial details.

content_sections.title: Single-Stage Object Detection: YOLO
content_sections.content:
Unlike two-stage detectors (e.g. Faster R-CNN) which generate region proposals and then classify them, single-stage detectors like **YOLO** (You Only Look Once) frame object detection as a single regression problem.

### 1. Grid Cell Division
The input image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid. If an object's center falls inside a grid cell, that grid cell is responsible for detecting that object.

### 2. Bounding Box Representation
Each grid cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes. Each bounding box is represented by 5 values:
*   $x, y$: Coordinates of the center of the box relative to the grid cell boundaries (bounded in $[0, 1]$).
*   $w, h$: Width and height of the box relative to the entire image.
*   $c$: The confidence score (or objectness score) reflecting the probability that the box contains an object, multiplied by the IoU between the predicted box and the ground truth:

$$p_{\text{obj}} = P(\text{Object}) \cdot \text{IoU}_{\text{pred}}^{\text{truth}}$$

Additionally, each cell predicts $C$ class probabilities $P(\text{Class}_i | \text{Object})$. The total output tensor size is $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$.

callouts.title: YOLO Loss Formulation
callouts.content: YOLO uses a multi-part sum-squared loss that penalizes bounding box coordinates, objectness, and class classifications. To stabilize training, coordinate loss is scaled up (using a hyperparameter $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$) and objectness confidence loss for cells containing no objects is scaled down (using $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$).

content_sections.title: Anchor Boxes, Intersection over Union (IoU), and NMS
content_sections.content:
To handle multiple overlapping objects and varied shapes within the same grid cell, detectors use **Anchor Boxes** and post-processing algorithms.

### 1. Anchor Boxes
Anchor boxes are pre-defined bounding box shapes (ratios and sizes) selected based on dataset statistics (using K-Means clustering). The model learns to predict offset values (deltas) relative to these anchor shapes, accelerating convergence.

### 2. Intersection over Union (IoU)
IoU measures the overlap between two bounding boxes $A$ and $B$. It is computed as the area of intersection divided by the area of union:

$$\text{IoU}(A, B) = \frac{\text{Area}(A \cap B)}{\text{Area}(A \cup B)} = \frac{\text{Area}(A \cap B)}{\text{Area}(A) + \text{Area}(B) - \text{Area}(A \cap B)}$$

### 3. Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)
During inference, the model outputs many overlapping candidate boxes for a single object. NMS filters these down using this protocol:
1.  Discard all boxes with an objectness score below a threshold (e.g. $0.5$).
2.  Select the remaining box with the highest objectness score and save it as a final prediction.
3.  Calculate the IoU of this box with all other remaining boxes of the same class.
4.  Discard any box with an $\text{IoU} \ge \text{NMS Threshold}$ (e.g. $0.45$).
5.  Repeat steps 2-4 until no candidate boxes remain.

content_sections.title: Semantic Segmentation & U-Net Architecture
content_sections.content:
Semantic Segmentation requires classifying every individual pixel in an image rather than outputting bounding boxes.

### 1. Symmetrical Sizing (U-Net)
U-Net consists of two symmetrical paths that form a U-shape:
*   **Contracting Path (Encoder):** A standard convolutional network that extracts high-level semantic features and downsamples spatial resolution (using Max Pooling).
*   **Expanding Path (Decoder):** A path that upsamples the spatial resolution (using transpose convolutions) to output a segmentation map matching the input image resolution.

### 2. Skip Connections
During spatial downsampling in the contracting path, fine spatial detail (exact coordinates of boundaries and edges) is lost. 
*   To recover this detail, U-Net uses **Skip Connections** that copy high-resolution feature maps from the contracting path and concatenate them with the corresponding upsampled features in the expanding path.
*   If the contracting path feature map at a level has shape $(C, H, W)$ and the upsampled feature map has shape $(C, H, W)$, concatenation yields a tensor of shape $(2C, H, W)$, combining spatial detail with semantic features.

callouts.title: Semantic vs. Instance Segmentation
callouts.content: Do not confuse semantic segmentation with instance segmentation. Semantic segmentation treats multiple objects of the same class as a single contiguous category (e.g. coloring all cars red). Instance segmentation identifies and separates every individual object instance (e.g. coloring different cars in different shades).
